Curriculum → Data storage and indexing → Choosing the right storage mix

Lesson 5 of 5

Choosing the right storage mix

Most serious systems use more than one storage technology. The skill is choosing a small set whose strengths align with the workload and team.

25-35 min Data storage and indexing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

This lesson ties the previous ones together into a decision process that is easy to explain in interviews.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Start with the invariant Transactions often imply relational or strongly consistent stores2. Then match the access pattern Write-heavy append workloads fit logs and wide-column systems3. Keep the operational portfolio small Prefer one primary store plus purpose-built secondary systems

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing This lesson ties the previous ones together into a decision process that is easy to explain in interviews. 2. Start with the invariant Transactions often imply relational or strongly consistent stores3. Then match the access pattern Write-heavy append workloads fit logs and wide-column systems4. Keep the operational portfolio small Prefer one primary store plus purpose-built secondary systems
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

This lesson ties the previous ones together into a decision process that is easy to explain in interviews.

What to say first

  • Choose stores by invariant and access pattern.
  • Use object storage and search indexes as distinct tools.
  • Limit the number of storage systems the team must run.
2

Study section

Start with the invariant

Identify which data needs strict correctness, which needs flexible querying, which needs cheap large-object storage, and which needs fast derived retrieval.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Transactions often imply relational or strongly consistent stores
  • Large blobs belong in object storage, not a transactional row store
  • Search often deserves its own index or engine
3

Study section

Then match the access pattern

Look at cardinality, fan-out, query flexibility, update frequency, and retention policy to decide the storage engine and surrounding caches.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Write-heavy append workloads fit logs and wide-column systems
  • Read-heavy key lookups fit caches or key-value stores
  • Complex joins and reporting often favor relational models or warehouses
4

Study section

Keep the operational portfolio small

Every extra datastore multiplies migration, monitoring, backup, on-call, and developer education costs.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Prefer one primary store plus purpose-built secondary systems
  • Document ownership and synchronization clearly
  • Explain why not choosing a more exotic store is a feature

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Storage choices should follow access patterns and correctness needs, not labels.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Choose stores by invariant and access pattern.
  • Use object storage and search indexes as distinct tools.
  • Limit the number of storage systems the team must run.
  • Explain synchronization between primary and derived stores.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Adding a new datastore for every problem.
  • Using a search index as the source of truth.
  • Ignoring backup, migration, and operator expertise.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What storage mix would you choose for a social media product?
  • Why is object storage usually the right place for media?
  • How do you justify not introducing another specialized database?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Relational data modeling and indexing Data storage and indexingThe NoSQL landscape Data storage and indexing

Interactive topic lab

Storage mix simulator

The best storage answer is a portfolio with a small number of clearly-scoped systems. Explain why each store exists, which request path it owns, and what happens if you choose differently.

Start from the riskiest invariant, then fan out to derived views.Every extra datastore needs an owner, sync plan, and incident story.Not choosing a specialized database can be the mature decision.

How to explain it

High-signal talking points

  • Start from the riskiest invariant, then fan out to derived views.
  • Every extra datastore needs an owner, sync plan, and incident story.
  • Not choosing a specialized database can be the mature decision.

Interactive diagram

Small, explainable storage portfolio

Rendering diagram…

Tie each datastore to a narrow job and keep the synchronization path visible.

Worked example

Use multiple stores only where the workload shape truly changes

A social product needs user profiles, media uploads, a feed, search, and notifications with uneven traffic growth across each domain.

Recommended choice

Use a relational or document primary for user/account state, object storage for media, a feed-optimized store or cache layer for timelines, and a search index for retrieval.

Why it fits

  • Media belongs in cheap durable blob storage, not in the transactional database.
  • Search retrieval is a derived read model and should not become the write authority.
  • Feed fan-out and ranking usually need a different scaling strategy than account state.

If you chose differently

If you force everything into one general database, either media and search become awkward, or the core account workflows inherit unnecessary operational complexity.

Interview-ready takeaway

Interviewers like hearing “one primary truth plus purpose-built secondary systems” because it sounds operable, not trendy.

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Choosing the right storage mix

Use the simulator to see when primary, replica, shard, or queue pressure should change your storage story. Apply it to choosing the right storage mix so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Choosing the right storage mix" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around start with the invariant and watch how it interacts with then match the access pattern.This lesson ties the previous ones together into a decision process that is easy to explain in interviews.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • read p95
  • replica utilization
  • shard pressure
  • cache hit ratio

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 100000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 38000 rps · 10 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 120000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 4: database · 8500 rps · 20 ms
  • Line 5: database · 17000 rps · 16 ms
  • Line 6: database · 21000 rps · 18 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Choose stores by invariant and access pattern.
  • Use object storage and search indexes as distinct tools.
  • Limit the number of storage systems the team must run.

Compiled simulation topology

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Start with the invariant and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Choose stores by invariant and access pattern. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Then match the access pattern.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Choosing the right storage mix" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • What storage mix would you choose for a social media product?
  • Start with the invariant
  • Then match the access pattern
  • Choose stores by invariant and access pattern.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
